ibn-abbas: Abdullah ibn Abbas (RA), the cousin of the Prophet Muhammad ﷺ, was born three years before the Hijra and died in 68 AH. Nicknamed Hibr al-Ummah (the Scholar of the Ummah) and Tarjuman al-Quran (the Interpreter of the Quran), he narrated over 1,600 hadiths. The Prophet ﷺ made special supplication for him: 'O Allah, teach him wisdom and the interpretation of the Quran.' He became the foremost authority on Quranic exegesis among the Companions, and thousands of students traveled to learn from him. He is considered the founder of the tafsir tradition in Islam.
abu-said-al-khudri: Abu Said al-Khudri (RA), whose full name was Sa'd ibn Malik ibn Sinan al-Ansari, was born in Madinah and lived until 74 AH. He participated in twelve military expeditions with the Prophet ﷺ and became one of the most prolific transmitters of hadith, narrating over 1,170 traditions. He was particularly known for his detailed transmission of the Prophet's guidance on acts of worship, spiritual purification, and social conduct. He remained in Madinah throughout his life, teaching hadith and serving as a reference for the Companions and Successors on religious matters.
al-bara-ibn-azib: Al-Bara ibn Azib (RA) was a Companion from the Ansar of Madinah who participated in eighteen military expeditions alongside the Prophet ﷺ. Though he was initially too young to participate in the Battle of Badr — the Prophet ﷺ sent him back due to his young age — he later took part in all subsequent major campaigns. He narrated approximately 305 hadiths covering various aspects of worship, conduct, and the Prophet's character. He settled in Kufa after the Prophet's death and was highly regarded as a transmitter in Iraq.
al-bara-bin-azib: Al-Bara ibn Azib (RA) was a Companion from the Ansar of Madinah who participated in eighteen military expeditions with the Prophet ﷺ. Initially too young to join the Battle of Badr, he later participated in all major campaigns. He narrated approximately 305 hadiths and settled in Kufa after the Prophet's death, where he became a key transmitter of prophetic traditions in Iraq. His reports cover prayer, fasting, funeral rites, and other pillars of Islamic practice.
abdullah-bin-masud: Abdullah ibn Mas'ud (RA) was one of the earliest converts to Islam, embracing the faith while still a young man in Makkah. He was so close to the Prophet ﷺ that he was given permission to enter the Prophet's home without seeking permission and described as one who resembled the Prophet ﷺ most closely in his walk and conduct. He narrated 848 hadiths and was appointed by Umar (RA) as a teacher and judge in Kufa. He is particularly renowned for his Quranic recitation and is considered one of the four great authorities on the Quran among the Companions.
abu-wail: Abu Wail Shaqiq ibn Salama al-Asadi was a leading Successor (Tabi'i) from Kufa who lived to approximately 82 AH. Though he missed seeing the Prophet ﷺ, he narrated hadiths from major Companions including Abdullah ibn Mas'ud, Ali ibn Abi Talib, Hudhayfa ibn al-Yaman, and Abu Musa al-Ash'ari. He was among the most reliable and trusted transmitters in Kufa and a major link in the isnad chains connecting the Companions to the next generation. Scholars of hadith relied on his transmission extensively in the Six Major Collections.
anas-ra: Anas ibn Malik (RA) was the personal servant of the Prophet Muhammad ﷺ for ten years, beginning when he was ten years old when his mother brought him to serve the Prophet in Madinah. He is one of the most prolific narrators of hadith, transmitting over 2,200 traditions. The Prophet ﷺ made supplication for him: 'O Allah, give him wealth and children and bless him therein.' He lived to approximately 93 AH, becoming the last surviving major Companion in Basra, where he narrated to thousands of students. His long life and personal proximity to the Prophet made him an irreplaceable source of prophetic knowledge.
adi-bin-hatim: Adi ibn Hatim al-Ta'i (RA) was initially a Christian Arab from the tribe of Tayy before embracing Islam around 9 AH. He was the son of the legendary Arab chieftain Hatim al-Ta'i, famous for his generosity. After accepting Islam, Adi ibn Hatim became a devoted Companion and narrated approximately 66 hadiths from the Prophet ﷺ. He participated in the early Islamic conquests and lived to approximately 68 AH, settling in Kufa. He was known for his loyalty to Ali (RA) during the period of civil strife and remained a respected elder figure in Iraq.
abu-masud: Abu Mas'ud al-Ansari (RA), whose full name was Uqba ibn Amr al-Ansari al-Badri, was from the Ansar of Madinah and was present at the Second Pledge of Aqabah. He participated in the Battle of Badr — a distinction that earned him the epithet 'al-Badri.' He narrated approximately 103 hadiths from the Prophet ﷺ covering prayer, charity, the Day of Judgment, and the Prophet's character. He later settled in Kufa where he served as an important early authority on hadith and Islamic rulings. He died during the caliphate of Muawiya.
as-saib-bin-yazid: As-Sa'ib ibn Yazid (RA) was a Companion who, according to his own account, was brought before the Prophet ﷺ as a young child and the Prophet ﷺ passed his hand over his head in blessing. He participated in Hajj with the Prophet ﷺ at the age of seven during the Farewell Pilgrimage. He went on to serve in the administration during the caliphate of Umar (RA) and later Uthman (RA). He narrated hadiths relating to the Prophet's physical description, conduct, and acts of worship. He lived to approximately 91 AH and was one of the last Companions to survive in Madinah.
asma: Asma bint Abi Bakr (RA) was the elder sister of Aisha (RA) and the daughter of Abu Bakr al-Siddiq (RA). She played a crucial role in the Prophet's migration to Madinah by carrying food to the Prophet ﷺ and her father as they hid in the Cave of Thawr. She is known as 'Dhat al-Nitaqayn' — the Lady of the Two Girdles — because she tore her girdle to tie the provisions for the Hijra. She narrated approximately 58 hadiths and lived to 100 years of age, dying shortly after the death of her son Abdullah ibn al-Zubayr in 73 AH.
uqba-bin-amir: Uqba ibn Amir al-Juhani (RA) was a Companion who embraced Islam in the early Medinan period and participated in numerous expeditions. He is known for his expertise in archery — the Prophet ﷺ encouraged Muslims to learn archery and Uqba was particularly skilled. He served as the governor of Egypt during the caliphate of Muawiya (RA) from 44–47 AH. He narrated approximately 55 hadiths relating to prayer, archery, conduct, and the virtues of reciting the Quran. He died in Egypt around 58 AH and is buried there.
ikrimah: Ikrimah was a leading Successor (Tabi'i) and the freed slave of Abdullah ibn Abbas (RA), from whom he learned the Quran and its interpretation extensively. He became one of the foremost authorities on Quranic exegesis of his generation, traveling widely across the Islamic world and narrating from numerous Companions. He is considered an important link in many chains of Quranic tafsir. Some scholars had reservations about his reliability in certain transmitted reports, but the majority of hadith scholars accepted his narrations, and he is found extensively in the collections of Abu Dawud, Tirmidhi, and Ibn Majah.
ata: Ata ibn Abi Rabah was a leading Successor (Tabi'i) who lived from approximately 27–114 AH and was the foremost jurist of Makkah in his era. He was an Abyssinian freed slave who became one of the most respected scholars of his time. He narrated from numerous Companions including Ibn Abbas, Ibn Umar, and Aisha (RA). Imam al-Shafi'i said about him: 'Ata was from those whose word was trusted in matters of religion.' He served as the chief Mufti of Makkah and is extensively cited in the Sunan collections on matters of Hajj, prayer, and fiqh.
alqama: Alqama ibn Qays al-Nakha'i was a leading Successor (Tabi'i) and major jurist of Kufa, dying approximately 62 AH. He was the most prominent student of Abdullah ibn Mas'ud (RA) and transmitted extensively from him, as well as from Umar, Ali, and Abu Mas'ud al-Ansari (RA). He was known for his deep knowledge of the Quran and fiqh and became the chief scholar of Kufa after Ibn Mas'ud's death. Ibrahim al-Nakha'i — who was his nephew — transmitted from him extensively, and through this chain, much of the Kufan jurisprudential tradition was preserved and passed to later generations.
aishah-may-allah-be-pleased-with-her: Aisha bint Abi Bakr (RA) was the beloved wife of the Prophet Muhammad ﷺ and the daughter of Abu Bakr al-Siddiq (RA). She is one of the most prolific narrators of hadith, transmitting over 2,210 traditions, and is regarded as a foundational source for knowledge of the Prophet's private conduct and household life. The Prophet ﷺ said: 'Take half of your religion from this Humaira (Aisha).' She was renowned for her intelligence, memory, and deep understanding of Islamic jurisprudence. After the Prophet's death, the Companions would refer to her when they needed clarification on religious matters. She died in 57 AH.
aisha-and-ibn-abbas: This dual attribution refers to a hadith reported jointly on the authority of both Aisha (RA) and Ibn Abbas (RA), two of the most prolific and scholarly Companions. Aisha (RA) narrated over 2,200 hadiths as the Prophet's wife and a leading jurist, while Ibn Abbas (RA), the Prophet's cousin, narrated over 1,600 hadiths and is known as Tarjuman al-Quran. When a hadith is attributed to both narrators, it indicates parallel transmission of the same prophetic statement through two independent chains, which strengthens the authenticity and transmission of the report.
abdur-rahman-bin-abi-laila: Abdur-Rahman ibn Abi Layla was a Successor (Tabi'i) and jurist of Kufa who died approximately 83 AH. His father, Abi Layla, was a Companion of the Prophet ﷺ. Abdur-Rahman narrated from numerous Companions including Ali ibn Abi Talib, Muadh ibn Jabal, Umar ibn al-Khattab, and others. He became a major scholarly figure in Kufa and was appointed as a judge (qadi) there. He is cited in various chapters of the Sunan collections and is considered a reliable narrator. He was present at several major battles during the period of the early caliphate.
um-atiya: Umm Atiyya al-Ansariyya (RA) was a Companion and skilled woman of the Ansar who participated in the burial washing of women who died during the Prophet's time, including Zaynab, the daughter of the Prophet ﷺ. The Prophet ﷺ trusted her with these important funeral duties. She narrated several hadiths about the rules of washing the deceased, funeral prayer, and related matters, making her one of the most important sources for Islamic funeral jurisprudence. Her reports are found in both Sahih al-Bukhari and Sahih Muslim and are relied upon in all four madhabs.
